system.description: Rulers and officers enjoy the intellectual stimulation of a variety of strategic boardgames. The listed price is for a basic wooden board with bone or stone pieces. Vastly more expensive boardgames may feature inlaid woods, precious metals, and gem- carved pieces. Historical examples include the Egyptian senet (passing), Germanic hnefatafl (kings table), Indian chaturanga (game of four divisions), Roman latrones (game of brigands), and Sumerian ur (game of twenty squares). In the Auran Empire, the most popular boardgame is kyrpeges (wargame).
